title: adhd_traits_not_personality_flaws

slides.headline: These aren't personality flaws. They're ADHD traits.
slides.body:
You've probably spent years thinking you were lazy, forgetful, too sensitive, or just bad at life.

You weren't. Your brain was working differently all along.

Scroll through — you'll recognize yourself. 🔖
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A soft cluster of small glowing sparks and gentle stars drifting upward against a warm cream background, surrounded by delicate botanical sprigs.

slides.headline: Object Permanence
slides.body:
ADHD brains often struggle with 'out of sight, out of mind' — not just for objects, but for tasks, people, and intentions. If you can't see it, your brain may act like it doesn't exist.

What it might look like:
🧺 You forget laundry in the machine until it smells, every single time
💊 Medication sits untouched because the bottle is inside a cabinet
📱 You lose touch with close friends for months — not because you don't care, but because they weren't in front of you
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A cozy cluttered desk seen from above with scattered papers, a closed notebook, a small plant, and a forgotten cup of tea, all rendered in soft muted tones.

slides.headline: Low Interoception
slides.body:
Interoception is your brain's ability to read signals from inside your body. Many people with ADHD have a reduced connection to these signals, making it hard to notice basic needs until they become urgent.

What it might look like:
🍽️ You realize you're dizzy and shaking before you notice you're hungry
🚽 You've held it for two hours because you genuinely didn't feel the urge to use the bathroom
😴 You crash into exhaustion without any warning — your body never seemed to send a 'getting tired' signal
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A soft cozy corner of a room with a rumpled blanket on a chair, an unfinished glass of water on a side table, and a small potted plant in gentle afternoon light.

slides.headline: Rejection Masking
slides.body:
Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria (RSD) is an intense emotional response to perceived criticism or rejection that is neurologically common in ADHD. To cope, many people learn to mask it — hiding the pain behind people-pleasing, over-explaining, or withdrawing.

What it might look like:
😶 A friend replies with a short text and you spend an hour convinced they're angry at you
✅ You say yes to things you hate because the idea of someone being disappointed feels unbearable
🚪 You go completely quiet after any hint of criticism rather than risk showing how much it stings
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly lit window with sheer curtains gently diffusing pale morning light, a small vase of simple flowers on the sill, casting a calm and quiet mood.

slides.headline: Time Blindness
slides.body:
ADHD affects the brain's ability to perceive and manage time. Research by Dr. Russell Barkley describes it as living in two time zones: 'now' and 'not now.' Planning for 'not now' is genuinely neurologically harder.

What it might look like:
⏰ You mean to leave in 10 minutes and suddenly 45 have passed and you have no idea where they went
📅 A deadline two weeks away feels so far off that your brain treats it as irrelevant — until it's tomorrow
🌀 You're late even when you tried your hardest and left earlier than usual, because transitions take more time than you account for
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A simple round analog clock face rendered in soft watercolor with blurred pastel hands, surrounded by small floating botanical leaves and a hazy dreamy background.

slides.headline: Emotional Permanence
slides.body:
Just as ADHD affects object permanence, it can affect emotional permanence — the ability to hold onto the felt sense of a relationship or positive emotion when it isn't actively present. This isn't emotional immaturity. It's a working memory gap.

What it might look like:
💛 You know logically that your partner loves you, but when they're not around you can't emotionally feel it and anxiety creeps in
🌧️ One bad interaction can completely overwrite a whole good day in your emotional memory
📞 You need more frequent reassurance in relationships and feel ashamed about it, not knowing it has a neurological basis
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. An open soft-cover journal with blank pages lying on a wooden surface beside a small sprig of dried lavender and a gentle warm pool of light.

slides.headline: You're not broken. Your brain just works differently.
slides.body:
Every single trait on this list was probably explained to you as a character flaw at some point.

It wasn't. It isn't.

Understanding your brain is the first step to actually working with it.

Save this for the moments you forget. 🔖
Follow @bloomfocus for more honest, science-backed ADHD content every week.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A single small potted plant with soft rounded leaves sitting in warm gentle light on a plain surface, rendered in calming sage green and cream tones with a peaceful, grounded feeling.

caption:
You didn't have a bad attitude. You had an unrecognized ADHD brain.

These 5 traits — from time blindness to emotional permanence — are neurologically documented patterns that millions of people grew up believing were personal failures. They weren't character flaws. They were clues.

Understanding the 'why' behind your behavior doesn't excuse it — it gives you somewhere real to start.

Which one surprised you most, or hit the hardest? Tell us in the comments. 👇

Save this and follow @bloomfocus for daily ADHD education that actually respects your brain.

title: emotional-dysregulation-feelings-hit-twice-as-hard

slides.headline: Why Your Feelings Hit Twice as Hard With ADHD
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A single small spark blooming into a large soft radiant burst of light, surrounded by gentle floating dots and soft glowing rings, on a warm cream background.

slides.headline: This is not a character flaw.
slides.body: Emotional dysregulation is a core feature of ADHD — not a personality problem. Your brain is wired differently, not broken.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly illustrated brain shape rendered in gentle lavender and blush tones, with small delicate blooming flowers growing from its surface, set against a warm cream background.

slides.headline: Your brain's brakes are undersized.
slides.body: The prefrontal cortex regulates emotional response. In ADHD brains, this region is underactive — meaning feelings arrive fast and loud, with less built-in filtering.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly illustrated brain shape with one section highlighted in a warm blush glow, surrounded by small radiating lines suggesting intensity, gentle and non-alarming, on a cream background.

slides.headline: Dopamine shapes how hard it lands.
slides.body: Low dopamine makes neutral moments feel flat and emotional ones feel overwhelming. Your nervous system is not overreacting — it is compensating for chronic underregulation.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. Two soft glowing orbs side by side — one small and dim, one larger and warmly luminous — surrounded by tiny floating particles, illustrated in a gentle dreamy style on a lavender-tinted cream background.

slides.headline: Rejection hits like a physical thing.
slides.body: Rejection Sensitive Dysphoria is real. A critical tone, an unanswered text, a small perceived slight — the emotional pain can feel instant and crushing, even when others don't understand why.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly lit window with gentle rain droplets on the glass, warm golden light filtering through sheer curtains, a small potted plant on the windowsill, calm and introspective mood.

slides.headline: Recovery takes longer than the trigger did.
slides.body: Once flooded, the ADHD brain struggles to self-soothe quickly. You might feel fine one moment and wrecked the next — and need real time to return to baseline. That is biology, not drama.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A cozy armchair beside a small side table with a steaming mug, a soft folded blanket draped over the arm, warm ambient light, illustrated in a gentle inviting style on a cream background.

slides.headline: You deserve support, not just strategies.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A small potted plant in a round terracotta pot, leaves rendered in soft sage green, sitting on a warm cream surface with a gentle soft shadow beneath it, simple and tender.

caption: Your feelings are not too much. Your brain just processes emotion differently — and there is real science behind why. In ADHD, emotional dysregulation is not about being sensitive or dramatic. It is a neurological reality rooted in how the prefrontal cortex and dopamine system work together. Understanding why your feelings hit so hard is the first step to being gentler with yourself on the hard days. Save this for the next time someone tells you you are overreacting. Save this and follow for daily ADHD content.

title: hidden_adhd_struggles_no_one_talks_about

slides.headline: 5 Hidden ADHD Struggles Nobody Talks About
slides.body:
You have probably felt these your whole life and had no name for them.

Scroll through — you will recognize yourself. Save this for the next time someone says you are just being lazy. 🔖
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly glowing brain shape surrounded by small floating question marks and gentle sparks, set against a cream background.

slides.headline: Waiting Mode
slides.body:
When you have one thing coming up later, your brain cannot start anything else until it is over. The whole day gets held hostage by a single appointment.

What it might look like:
🪑 You have a 3pm call so you sit doing nothing useful from 10am onward, even though you have hours free
📋 You cannot start a project because you are waiting on one small piece of information first
🚗 You arrive 45 minutes early rather than risk the anxiety of the wait back home
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A large soft clock face with gentle hands frozen mid-tick, surrounded by small floating hourglasses on a warm cream background.

slides.headline: Decision Paralysis
slides.body:
ADHD brains struggle to rank options by importance, so even tiny choices can cause a full system freeze. It is not indecisiveness — it is a working memory and executive function issue.

What it might look like:
🍕 You spend 40 minutes trying to pick what to eat and then just do not eat
📧 You draft an email five times, cannot decide if the tone is right, and close the tab without sending it
🛒 You abandon a full online shopping cart because choosing between two similar items feels impossible
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. An open journal with two blank pages side by side, a pencil resting in the middle as if stuck between them, small soft arrows pointing in different directions around it.

slides.headline: Auditory Processing Lag
slides.body:
You heard the words, but by the time your brain finished processing them, the conversation has already moved on. ADHD affects how quickly the brain converts sound into meaning, completely separate from hearing ability.

What it might look like:
👂 Someone gives you a three-step instruction and you can only recall the last step by the time they finish
😶 You say 'yes' to something in a meeting and then have absolutely no idea what you agreed to
📺 You watch TV with subtitles on not because of volume but because your brain needs the visual backup
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A small vintage telephone handset with soft sound wave lines radiating out from it, the waves gently fading before they reach the edges of the frame, on a lavender background.

slides.headline: The Wall of Awful
slides.body:
Before starting a task, ADHD brains can experience an intense wall of negative emotion — dread, shame, boredom, overwhelm — built up from every previous bad experience with that task. The task itself is not the barrier. The emotional weight of it is.

What it might look like:
💌 Replying to one simple text feels like climbing a mountain because unanswered messages have piled up with shame
🧺 You cannot start the laundry because the last three loads sat in the machine too long and you feel too guilty to try again
📑 You avoid opening your emails for days because the backlog feels like a personal failure you are not ready to face
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A cozy messy desk scene with stacked papers, a tipped-over pencil cup, and a small wilting plant beside a steaming mug, all rendered in soft warm tones.

slides.headline: Hyperfixation Crash
slides.body:
After an intense period of hyperfocus, your brain can hit a wall of complete exhaustion, flatness, and inability to feel interest in anything at all. The high always has a comedown.

What it might look like:
🛋️ You spend a weekend deep in a new hobby and then feel zero desire to touch it again for two weeks
😶‍🌫️ After finishing a big project you feel empty and unmotivated rather than proud or relieved
📚 You read every article on a topic for days straight, then feel almost nothing about it the next week and cannot explain why
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A cozy unmade bed with rumpled soft blankets and a few open books scattered across it, gentle morning light coming from the side, pillows slightly squashed, calm and quiet mood.

slides.headline: You are not broken.
slides.body:
Your brain is not lazy. It is not dramatic. It is not making excuses.

These are real, documented, neurological experiences that millions of people with ADHD live with every single day — usually in silence.

Naming them is the first step to working with your brain instead of against it.

Save this for the hard days. 🔖
Follow @bloomfocus for more science-backed ADHD content that actually feels human.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A small potted plant with soft rounded leaves sitting on a sunny windowsill, gentle light casting a warm glow around it, a single small bloom opening at the top.

caption:
Your brain is not lazy — it is just doing things nobody gave you the language for. 🧠

Waiting Mode, the Wall of Awful, Auditory Processing Lag — these are real neurological patterns that come with ADHD, and most people spend decades thinking they are just bad at life because of them.

Naming what is happening in your brain does not fix everything, but it is a genuinely powerful place to start.

Which one surprised you most — or hit a little too close to home? Drop it in the comments. 👇

Save this and follow @bloomfocus for daily ADHD content that is science-backed and actually warm about it.

title: 7_types_executive_function_adhd

slides.headline: ADHD isn't laziness. It's an executive function problem.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A softly glowing brain shape nestled among small pastel stars and gentle swirling lines, floating in a calm lavender space.

slides.headline: 1. Working Memory: your brain's sticky note
slides.body: ADHD shrinks working memory capacity. That's why you walk into a room and forget why, or lose your train of thought mid-sentence. It's neurological, not carelessness.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A cozy cluttered desk with scattered pastel sticky notes, a small plant, and an open notebook, lit by warm soft light.

slides.headline: 2. Inhibition: braking when your brain says go
slides.body: ADHD weakens the brain's brake system. Impulsive spending, blurting things out, jumping between tasks — that's poor inhibitory control, not bad character.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. Soft scattered sparks and small pastel lightning-like bursts radiating outward from a central gentle glow, on a cream background.

slides.headline: 3. Task Initiation: starting is the hardest part
slides.body: ADHD brains need dopamine to launch. Without it, starting a task — even one you want to do — can feel physically impossible. This is called activation dysfunction.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A single warm coffee cup on a small saucer, steam curling upward gently, sitting on a quiet pale surface with a soft blush shadow beneath.

slides.headline: 4. Cognitive Flexibility: switching mental gears
slides.body: ADHD makes shifting between tasks, plans, or ideas genuinely hard. Unexpected changes can feel overwhelming. This isn't rigidity — it's a dysregulated transition system.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A gentle winding path splitting softly into two directions, surrounded by small sage green leaves and tiny blush wildflowers, in a calm open landscape.

slides.headline: 5. Emotional Regulation: feelings hit harder
slides.body: ADHD brains experience emotions more intensely and struggle to dial them back. Rejection sensitivity, frustration spirals, sudden mood crashes — all linked to this underrated piece of EF.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. Soft diffused light streaming through a tall window, casting gentle warm shapes across a plain cream interior wall, with a small potted plant on the sill.

slides.headline: 6 & 7. Planning + Time Management: the double hit
slides.body: ADHD impairs both building a plan and sensing time passing. 'Time blindness' is real — future deadlines don't feel urgent until they're on top of you. This is your prefrontal cortex, not your priorities.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A simple round analog clock face with soft lavender hands and no numerals, resting gently on a cream surface beside a small open journal and a sprig of greenery.

slides.headline: You were never just not trying hard enough.
slides.body: Save this for the next time someone — or the voice in your head — says otherwise. Follow @bloomfocus for more honest, science-backed ADHD education.
slides.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Portrait 4:5 composition. A small potted plant with rounded sage green leaves sitting in a blush pink ceramic pot, centered on a warm cream surface with soft gentle shadow.

caption: Your ADHD brain isn't broken — it's running on a different operating system. Executive function is the set of brain-based skills that manage how we plan, start, focus, shift, remember, and regulate ourselves. ADHD directly impairs all seven of them, and that explains so much more than just 'trouble focusing.' Swipe through to finally understand what's actually going on in your brain — and send this to someone who needs to see it. Save this and follow for daily ADHD content.
